content.text: What it is

A bug severity and priority matrix is a two-dimensional classification tool that maps how bad a bug is (severity) against how urgently it must be fixed (priority). In conventional software, both axes collapse together most of the time: a crash is both severe and urgent. In agentic AI systems the axes decouple: a hallucination might be severe to the user who experienced it but low-priority for the team if it occurs in fewer than 0.01% of requests and is non-reproducible.

The matrix formalizes four severity levels (S1–S4) and four priority levels (P1–P4) and defines rules for mapping one to the other, including AI-specific modifiers that adjust the default mapping based on reproducibility, affected-user count, and safety adjacency.
Why it matters

Conventional bug triage matrices are built for deterministic software. When you label a null-pointer exception as S2/P1, the fix is a code change that reliably removes the crash path. AI bugs violate that assumption in three ways.

First, AI bugs are often stochastic: the same input does not always produce the wrong output. A bug that manifests on 0.3% of requests still needs a severity label, but its priority should reflect that frequency. Second, the impact surface is harder to bound: a prompt injection bug that affects one workflow may silently affect all workflows using the same system prompt. Third, the fix may be a model update, a prompt change, or a guardrail threshold adjustment — each with different release cycle latency. A matrix purpose-built for AI makes these distinctions explicit.
Benefits
Gives every team member a shared vocabulary for describing AI issues without ambiguity.
Prevents S1 issues from sitting in a backlog because someone labeled them P3 by default.
Allows non-reproducible hallucinations to receive lower priority without being dismissed.
Provides a paper trail for compliance: when a guardrail miscalibration is reported, the severity label documents how seriously the team took it.
Makes SLA commitments to customers concrete: S1 issues are resolved within four hours; S4 issues are addressed in the next sprint.
Separates the question of severity (how bad is it?) from priority (when do we fix it?), which is critical when the engineering capacity to fix is constrained.
Trade-offs
Classification overhead: every new bug must be assessed before it gets a label, which adds time to intake. Automated pre-labeling (see Tooling section) partially offsets this.
AI-specific modifiers add complexity. Teams new to the matrix may argue about whether a hallucination that affects 2% of users is S2 or S3.
The matrix does not substitute for a root-cause analysis. A P1 label signals urgency, not diagnosis.
Priority can be gamed: teams under sprint pressure may down-classify bugs. Requiring a second reviewer for S1/S2 labels mitigates this.
The matrix must be versioned when the AI system's safety envelope changes. A threshold that defines S1 today may need revision after a model upgrade.
How it works
Severity measures user impact — what the bug does to the experience or to safety. Priority measures urgency — when the team must act. The matrix combines them with a set of rules and modifiers.

S1 is reserved for safety issues (harmful output, PII exposure, data breach) and complete service outages. S2 covers significant quality degradation visible to many users or any SLA breach. S3 covers quality degradation affecting some users or a feature being partially broken. S4 is cosmetic issues and narrow edge cases.
P1 means interrupt current work and fix now. P2 means fix in the current sprint. P3 means fix in the next sprint. P4 means fix when convenient.

A non-reproducible bug (hallucination that cannot be triggered reliably) drops one priority level from the default. A safety-adjacent bug (output that is not harmful today but could be under a slightly different prompt) raises one priority level. Bugs affecting more than 20% of users in their category raise one priority level.
Architecture

attrs.alt: AI Bug Severity and Priority Matrix: S1-S4 severity rows crossed with P1-P4 priority columns, showing required, conditional, and not-allowed cells with AI-specific examples

content.text: The matrix is read row-first. Find the severity row that best describes the user impact, then move right to find the default priority column. Apply modifiers to shift left (raise urgency) or right (lower urgency). S1 bugs are always P1 regardless of modifiers. No bug with severity S1 or S2 is permitted to land at P4.
When to use it
At bug intake: whenever a new issue is filed, the DRI assigns an S/P label before the issue enters the backlog.
During triage: weekly or daily triage meetings review unlabeled issues and confirm or adjust labels set at intake.
At escalation: when a customer reports an issue in production, the matrix determines whether it triggers an incident or a standard sprint item.
During post-mortems: the matrix provides a retrospective record of how an issue was originally classified versus how serious it turned out to be.
When negotiating SLAs: the matrix makes explicit what response time is committed for each severity tier.
How to implement
Define your severity thresholds in writing. For S1, enumerate the specific conditions: harmful output, PII in response, complete service outage, security breach. This prevents ambiguity.

Define your AI-specific bug types and their default severity: hallucination bugs start at S3 unless they cause harm; configuration bugs (bad system prompt) start at S2 if widespread; model regression bugs inherit the severity of the degraded capability.

Build a bug template that forces the reporter to fill in: (a) affected user count or percentage, (b) is the bug reproducible with a fixed input?, (c) does the output touch a safety-relevant topic?, and (d) was this caused by a model update, a prompt change, or an infrastructure change?

Wire the template fields into an automatic pre-label step. A simple heuristic: if 'harmful output' is checked, pre-label S1/P1; if affected users > 20%, pre-label S2; otherwise S3. The DRI confirms or overrides.
Document the SLA for each S/P combination and publish it to all stakeholders.
Review the matrix thresholds whenever the system's capabilities or user base changes significantly.
Examples

# Bug template fields for AI issues
bug_template:
  title: ""
  type:
    options: [hallucination, regression, configuration, infrastructure, eval]
  affected_pct: 0.0
  reproducible: false
  harmful_output: false
  pii_in_response: false
  safety_adjacent: false
  caused_by: ""   # model_update | prompt_change | infra_change | unknown
  severity: ""    # auto-labeled, DRI confirms
  priority: ""    # auto-labeled, DRI confirms
  sla_deadline: "" # computed from severity
Real-world example

A customer support agent built on an LLM starts generating responses that include the phrase 'I cannot provide legal advice' in answer to questions about product return policies. The behavior was introduced by a system-prompt change that added an overly broad legal disclaimer.

The bug is filed. The reporter notes: affected users = 100% of return-policy queries (roughly 8% of total), reproducible with fixed input, no harmful output, no PII, safety-adjacent (the phrase implies liability exposure). The pre-labeler assigns S2/P2. The DRI reviews and bumps to S2/P1 because the system-prompt root cause is confirmed and the fix is a one-line change — the cost of the fix is low, so the marginal cost of waiting a sprint is not justified. The fix is deployed within the day.
Common mistakes
Labeling all hallucinations as S1. A hallucination that states an incorrect product feature version is S3, not S1. Reserve S1 for output that could cause real-world harm.
Setting priority based on fix difficulty rather than user impact. A hard-to-fix S2 bug is still P1 or P2; the team needs more resources, not a lower priority label.
Forgetting the reproducibility modifier. A non-reproducible bug that would be S2/P1 if reproducible may reasonably be S2/P2 or S2/P3 if the team cannot trigger it.
Using one matrix across deterministic and AI components without distinguishing bug types. A null-pointer exception in the tool-calling layer is a code bug; classify it with conventional rules.
Not reviewing severity thresholds after model upgrades. A model that is more capable also has a different failure surface; thresholds calibrated for the old model may be wrong.
Allowing bugs to remain unlabeled in the backlog. Every bug must have an S/P label before it leaves intake; an unlabeled bug is invisible to triage.
Best practices
Require two approvals to assign S1. One person's judgment about safety is fallible; a second DRI must confirm.
Automate pre-labeling at intake using a structured template and a heuristic classifier. The DRI's job is then to review and override, not to classify from scratch.
Publish the SLA table in the team wiki and link to it from every S1/P1 ticket so owners know their deadline without looking it up.
Store the full rationale for each label in the bug, especially when a modifier was applied. Future reviewers need to understand why a S2 bug was given P3 priority.
Run a monthly calibration review: pick 10 recently resolved bugs and re-classify them blind. Disagreements reveal drift in team understanding of the thresholds.
Treat eval score drops as potential S2/S3 bugs. A 5% drop in task-completion rate on the regression eval suite is a quality degradation, even if no single user has reported it yet.
Performance and scalability

The matrix itself has no runtime overhead — it is a classification scheme applied at bug intake. At scale, the overhead is human time: at 100 bugs per week, a DRI spending two minutes per bug on S/P confirmation costs roughly three hours per week. Automated pre-labeling with a structured intake form reduces confirmation time to under 30 seconds for clear-cut cases.

For large AI platforms with multiple product lines, maintain one canonical matrix with product-specific severity sub-criteria. Avoid separate matrices per team: a hallucination is S3 regardless of which product it appeared in. What varies per product is the affected-user count threshold for S2 (a high-volume consumer product has different user count scales than an enterprise API).
Tooling and ecosystem
Linear, Jira, and GitHub Issues all support custom fields for severity and priority. Add them as required fields in your bug template.
Slack-based bots (e.g., a simple webhook) can post to a #triage channel whenever a new bug is filed without an S/P label, pinging the on-call DRI.
LLM-based intake classifiers can parse a bug description and suggest S/P labels. Use these as drafts for human review, not as final labels.
Eval frameworks (e.g., Evals by OpenAI, DeepEval, custom eval harnesses) can automatically open S3 bug tickets when a regression eval score crosses a threshold.
OpsGenie, PagerDuty, and similar on-call tools can be configured to page the on-call engineer whenever a P1 bug is created.
Comparison with alternatives
Approach
How it handles AI bugs
Key limitation
Generic S/P matrix
Applies standard code-bug thresholds
No modifier for non-reproducibility or safety adjacency
Impact-only triage
Labels bugs purely by user impact
Ignores urgency; everything becomes equally urgent
Risk-based severity
Severity = probability × impact
Requires probability estimates teams rarely have
AI bug severity matrix (this doc)
Adds AI-specific bug types and reproducibility modifier
Requires calibration as model and user base evolve
No formal triage
Ad hoc decisions
P1 bugs go unnoticed until customer escalation
Security and reliability

The S1 severity tier must include all security-relevant failure modes: prompt injection that exfiltrates context, PII leakage in responses, and output that could be used to harm users. Security bugs bypass the normal priority modifier rules — a security bug is always at least P1, regardless of affected-user count or reproducibility.

Reliability considerations: a P1 bug that sits unfixed for longer than its SLA deadline should trigger an automatic escalation to the engineering manager. Track SLA adherence as a metric alongside bug counts. If more than 10% of P1 bugs miss their SLA, the triage process or the team's capacity needs review.
Mental model

Think of severity as the height from which the bug drops a user (S1 = fell off a cliff, S4 = stubbed a toe). Think of priority as how quickly the team dispatches a rescue (P1 = immediate, P4 = scheduled maintenance visit). In deterministic software, a cliff fall always warrants immediate rescue. In AI, the cliff fall might be unconfirmed — someone reported a fall but you cannot reproduce the conditions. The matrix encodes that nuance: the fall is still severe (S1 if confirmed harmful), but without confirmation the rescue priority may be lower until the next triage session can investigate.

The reproducibility modifier is the key AI-specific insight: non-reproducible bugs are not less severe if they do happen, but the team cannot fix what it cannot trigger. Lower priority reflects limited actionability, not lower concern.
Key takeaways
Severity (impact) and priority (urgency) are separate axes. In AI systems they decouple more often than in deterministic software.
S1 is reserved for harmful output, security breaches, and complete outages. It is always P1.
AI-specific bug types — hallucination, regression, configuration, infrastructure, eval — each have a default severity starting point.
Non-reproducible bugs drop one priority level; safety-adjacent bugs raise one priority level.
Automate pre-labeling at intake to reduce DRI overhead to a review-and-confirm step.
Review the matrix thresholds after every significant model upgrade or user-base change.
Every bug must leave intake with an S/P label. Unlabeled bugs are invisible to triage and SLA tracking.
Severity × Priority Decision Matrix

attrs.alt: Bug severity vs priority matrix with SLA targets for GenAI services

content.text: Severity measures the technical impact of a bug (how broken is the system?), while priority measures the business urgency of fixing it (how quickly must it be fixed?). These are independent axes — a SEV-1 with no active users at 3am may have Priority 3, while a SEV-3 affecting a high-value enterprise customer demo may be Priority 1.
GenAI-Specific Severity Criteria
SEV-1 (Complete outage):
Agent produces no responses, all tool calls fail, hallucination rate >50% on benchmarks, PII leak confirmed, safety guardrails bypassed in production.
SEV-2 (Major degradation):
P95 latency >5× baseline, >20% of requests returning errors, specific tool class failing (e.g. all web search calls), wrong language output for >10% of users.
SEV-3 (Partial impact):
Degraded quality for a subset of inputs (specific prompt patterns), single non-critical tool failing, UI display artifacts in agent output formatting, minor token budget miscalculation.
SEV-4 (Minor/cosmetic):
Typos in system prompt that don't affect behavior, non-critical logging gaps, UI alignment issues in agent thought display, suboptimal (but correct) tool selection.
Triage Workflow

def triage_bug(report: dict) -> dict:
    """Assign severity and priority to an inbound bug report."""
    severity = assess_severity(
        error_rate=report.get('error_rate_pct', 0),
        users_affected=report.get('users_affected', 0),
        pii_exposure=report.get('pii_exposure', False),
        safety_bypass=report.get('safety_bypass', False),
    )
    priority = assess_priority(
        customer_tier=report.get('customer_tier', 'standard'),
        revenue_at_risk=report.get('revenue_at_risk_usd', 0),
        regulatory_exposure=report.get('regulatory_exposure', False),
        workaround_available=report.get('workaround_available', True),
    )
    sla = SLA_MATRIX[severity][priority]
    return {
        'severity': severity,
        'priority': priority,
        'response_sla': sla['response'],
        'resolve_sla': sla['resolve'],
        'escalate_to': sla['escalate_to'],
        'hotfix_required': severity == 'SEV1' and priority in ('P1', 'P2'),
    }

SLA_MATRIX = {
    'SEV1': {
        'P1': {'response': '15m', 'resolve': '4h',  'escalate_to': 'VP Eng'},
        'P2': {'response': '30m', 'resolve': '8h',  'escalate_to': 'Eng Manager'},
        'P3': {'response': '4h',  'resolve': '24h', 'escalate_to': 'Team Lead'},
        'P4': {'response': '1d',  'resolve': '1w',  'escalate_to': 'Team Lead'},
    },
    'SEV2': {
        'P1': {'response': '1h',  'resolve': '24h', 'escalate_to': 'Eng Manager'},
        'P2': {'response': '4h',  'resolve': '3d',  'escalate_to': 'Team Lead'},
        'P3': {'response': '1d',  'resolve': '1w',  'escalate_to': 'On-Call'},
        'P4': {'response': '3d',  'resolve': '2w',  'escalate_to': 'On-Call'},
    },
}
